title: Part-Time Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2000
chapters: SI 2000/1551 · Made 8 June 2000 · In Force 1 July 2000 · Implements Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC (Framework Agreement between ETUC, UNICE and CEEP)
desc: The Part-Time Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2000 provide that a part-time worker has the right not to be treated by the employer less favourably than the employer treats a comparable full-time worker in respect of the terms of their contract, or by being subjected to any other detriment, on the ground that the worker is a part-time worker, unless the treatment is objectively justified. The pro rata principle applies: a part-time worker is entitled to receive pay and benefits on a pro rata basis unless a different approach is more appropriate. The comparator must be employed by the same employer under the same type of contract, doing the same or broadly similar work, at the same establishment (or a different establishment if no comparator at the same one). Workers who become part-time or return part-time after absence are entitled not to be treated less favourably than they would have been treated had they remained full-time. Dismissal for asserting rights under the Regulations is automatically unfair. Part-time workers can also bring indirect sex discrimination claims under EqA 2010 s.19 where a requirement to work full-time disproportionately affects women.

parts.title: Less Favourable Treatment of Part-Time Workers

sections.title: Less favourable treatment of part-time workers
sections.summary: A part-time worker has the right not to be treated less favourably than a comparable full-time worker in respect of the terms of their contract or by being subjected to any other detriment, on the ground that they are a part-time worker, unless the less favourable treatment is justified on objective grounds. The pro rata principle applies unless it is inappropriate to do so. A written statement of reasons for less favourable treatment must be provided within 21 days of request (reg.6). The comparator must be employed by the same employer, under the same type of contract, engaged in the same or broadly similar work (having regard to qualifications, skills and experience).
sections.text:
(1) A part-time worker has the right not to be treated by his employer less favourably than the employer treats a comparable full-time worker—
(a) as regards the terms of his contract; or
(b) by being subjected to any other detriment by any act, or deliberate failure to act, of his employer.

(2) The right conferred by paragraph (1) applies only if—
(a) the treatment is on the ground that the worker is a part-time worker, and
(b) the treatment is not justified on objective grounds.

(3) In determining whether a part-time worker has been treated less favourably than a comparable full-time worker, the pro rata principle shall be applied unless it is inappropriate.

(4) A part-time worker paid at a lower rate for overtime worked by him in a period which does not exceed the number of weekly hours of work by a comparable full-time worker is not for that reason alone to be regarded as treated less favourably than the comparable full-time worker.

versions.description: Original commencement implementing Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC.

leadingCases.name: Matthews v Kent and Medway Towns Fire Authority [2006] UKHL 8
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords held that retained (part-time) firefighters could compare themselves with full-time firefighters for the purposes of the PTWR, even though their contracts differed in some respects. The question is whether the full-time and part-time workers are engaged in the same or broadly similar work, not whether their contracts are identical.
leadingCases.significance: Leading authority on the 'same or broadly similar work' comparator test for part-time workers

leadingCases.name: Carl v University of Sheffield [2009] ICR 1286
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that the requirement that the comparator be employed under the 'same type of contract' means the comparison must be like-for-like: a part-time employee can only compare with a full-time employee, and a part-time worker can only compare with a full-time worker. This prevents cross-status comparisons.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the 'same type of contract' requirement for the comparator

refs: Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC (Framework Agreement clause 4 — principle of non-discrimination)
reg.6 PTWR 2000 (right to written statement of reasons for less favourable treatment)
reg.7 PTWR 2000 (unfair dismissal and detriment protection)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect sex discrimination — refusal of part-time working may disproportionately affect women)
Dobson v North Cumbria Integrated Care NHS Foundation Trust [2021] UKEAT (full-time requirement as indirect sex discrimination)

pendingAmendments: Post-Brexit — PTWR 2000 now assimilated law under REUL Act 2023
No ERA 2025 amendments to PTWR directly, but flexible working reforms (EReFW Act 2023, ACAS Code 5) interact with part-time working requests

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: European Communities Act 1972 s.2(2) and Employment Relations Act 1999 s.19
parliamentaryOrigin.euDirective: Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC (Framework Agreement between ETUC, UNICE and CEEP)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Trade and Industry
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The PTWR 2000 implemented the Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC. The Directive's Framework Agreement established the principle of non-discrimination against part-time workers and required member states to remove unjustified barriers to part-time work. The UK extended the Regulations to cover workers (not just employees) from the outset, going beyond the minimum Directive requirement. Part-time workers can also bring indirect sex discrimination claims under EqA 2010 s.19 where a full-time requirement disproportionately affects women — this provides an alternative (and often more powerful) route to challenge less favourable treatment.

precedingDocuments.title: Part-Time Work Directive 97/81/EC
precedingDocuments.significance: The governing EU Directive incorporating the Framework Agreement on part-time work. Established the non-discrimination principle and required removal of barriers to part-time work.

precedingDocuments.title: Employment Relations Act 1999 s.19
precedingDocuments.significance: Provided additional enabling powers for part-time worker regulations alongside the ECA 1972.

leadingCases.name: Matthews v Kent and Medway Towns Fire Authority [2006] UKHL 8
leadingCases.heldText: Retained (part-time) firefighters could compare with full-time firefighters. The test is 'same or broadly similar work', not identical contracts.
leadingCases.significance: House of Lords authority on the comparator test for part-time workers.

leadingCases.name: Carl v University of Sheffield [2009] ICR 1286
leadingCases.heldText: The 'same type of contract' requirement means like-for-like comparison only — a part-time employee compares with a full-time employee, not a full-time worker.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the 'same type of contract' comparator requirement.

leadingCases.name: Sharma v Manchester City Council [2008] ICR 623
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that 'on the ground that the worker is a part-time worker' requires a causal connection between the part-time status and the less favourable treatment. The treatment must be because the worker is part-time, not merely correlated with it.
leadingCases.significance: Established the causation requirement — treatment must be because of part-time status.
